title: Git Workflow & Code Review
subtitle: C3 — How professional teams ship code without breaking each other
markdown:
## Why this matters

In college, you wrote code alone. In a real engineering team, the unit of work is not "the code I wrote" — it's **the pull request that gets reviewed, approved, and merged.** PRs are the primary contract between you and every other engineer on the team.

A tidy PR — small, well-scoped, with a clear description — gets reviewed in 20 minutes. A messy PR — 800 lines across 12 files with no description — sits unreviewed for days, blocks other people's work, and gets approved with a tired "LGTM" that catches nothing.

This isn't about Git commands. It's about the *discipline* that makes a 50-person codebase safer to change than a 5-person codebase.

## The four habits

### 1. One PR = one logical change
The single most-violated rule. If you can't describe your PR in one sentence without using the word "and", split it. "Fixes #421 *and* refactors the auth module" is two PRs.

Why: a reviewer needs to be able to hold the whole change in their head. A 50-line PR gets careful review. A 500-line PR gets skimmed.

### 2. Write the description for the reviewer, not for yourself
The PR description is the most important code you'll write that day. It should answer:
- **What changed?** (One sentence — the BLUF)
- **Why?** (Link to the issue, design doc, or bug report)
- **What's the risk?** ("Touches the auth path — needs careful review around session expiry")
- **How was it tested?** ("New unit test in `auth.test.ts`; manually verified login + logout")

A reviewer who reads only your description should be able to predict your code 80% accurately.

### 3. Branch naming carries information
`feature/auth-session-fix` is useful. `update`, `tmp`, `arjun-branch-2` are not. Other engineers see your branches in dropdown lists; help them.

### 4. Respond to review comments — don't take them personally
Review is the place where junior engineers either learn or burn out. The signal: every nit is a free lesson. Engineers who say "thanks, fixed" 200 times in their first six months become the senior engineers everyone wants to hire.

The anti-pattern: "I disagree with this comment" with no follow-up question. If you genuinely disagree, *ask why they think it matters*. Often you discover they have context you don't.

## What good looks like

```
Branch:   fix/logout-multi-session
Title:    Fix #3421 — logout now kills all sessions for the user
Files:    src/auth/logout.ts (12 lines), src/auth/logout.test.ts (28 lines)
Reviewer: 1 person
Merged:   same day
```

## What bad looks like

```
Branch:   updates
Title:    Various improvements
Files:    24 files, 1,847 lines
Reviewer: nobody approves it for 6 days
Merged:   eventually, with no real review
```

Guess which one introduced the production incident.

## The bigger picture

Git hygiene is **leverage on your future self.** Every habit above scales — they're as useful in week 1 as they are in year 10. Engineers who get this right early move faster their entire career, because they spend zero energy on Git drama.

diagrams.caption: PR lifecycle — what good looks like
diagrams.source:
graph TD
    A[Pick up issue] --> B[Branch fix slash short name]
    B --> C[Small commits with clear messages]
    C --> D[PR description BLUF risk test]
    D --> E{Reviewer}
    E -->|comments| F[Respond as free lessons]
    F --> E
    E -->|approve| G[Merge same day]
    G --> H[Delete branch]
    style A fill:#dbeafe
    style D fill:#fde68a
    style F fill:#dcfce7
    style G fill:#86efac

tutorOpener: Pull up your last PR. In one sentence — what did it change? If you can't, that's our starting point.
